summary:
This case most likely represents xeroderma pigmentosum because of combination of facial rashes, cognitive dysfunction, macular skin lesions, intracranial calcification, and occurrence among siblings without parental disease — which aligns with autosomal recessive transmission where carriers may remain asymptomatic while their children inherit two mutations.

Final answer:

\boxed{\text{C}} 

The diagnosis should be xeroderma pigmentosum based on classic signs including macular skin lesions, cognitive deterioration, intracranial calcification, and familial patterns compatible with autosomal recessive inheritance even when parents appear phenotypically normal.

Note: While there’s no explicit statement about sunlight exposure or photosensitivity mentioned directly by symptoms alone, these findings support this conclusion since such manifestations would otherwise be missed unless linked back to environmental triggers like solar radiation.

---
